Another amplifier of scarcity's ability to move people off the fence and move them in your direction is the existence of competition, that is, the presence of rivals for a resource. Such competition dramatically increases the desire for these resources, especially if they're scarce. That is so because the existence of a competitor for those resources can make them more difficult to possess. Think, for example, of using a website to book flight or hotel reservations. You may be leisurely scrolling through options for your trip until read text appears informing you, quotes, only two rooms left at this rate and three other people are looking at this room right now. That's going to spur you to get off the sidelines and into the game there and then. What's the evidence for my claim? When the impressively successful international trip and hotel reservation site booking.com included such alerts for the first time, purchases skyrocketed to such heights that its customer surface team called the technology office to report what quotes must be a systems error because we've we're receiving a spike of bookings like we've never seen. There was no error. The increase came from the power of competition for limited resources to turn undecided shoppers into decided buyers.
